content.problemStatement:
On a certain planet, there are nnn regions in a line, numbered from 111 to nnn. In the iiith of these regions resides a species where each organism has weight wiw_iwi​, as well as enough resources to support aia_iai​ organisms.

You are tasked with placing kkk walls to separate the planet into k+1k+1k+1 segments. Each wall should be placed between two regions iii and i+1i+1i+1, where 1≤i≤n−11 \leq i \leq n-11≤i≤n−1, and all walls should be placed in different positions. After you place the walls, the following happens in each segment:


The species with the highest wiw_iwi​ wins and kills all other species in the segment, thus taking control of the whole segment.
Over time, this species uses up all the resources of the segment, and thus the number of organisms of the winning species becomes equal to the sum of aia_iai​ in the segment.
Define the biomass\textit{biomass}biomass of the planet as the sum of the weights of all the organisms living on the planet. You don't like life, so your goal is to place the kkk walls to minimise\textit{minimise}minimise the biomass. What is the minimum value you can achieve?


Input Format

The first line contains two space-separated integers nnn and kkk, the number of regions and number of walls to be placed respectively.
The second line contains nnn space-separated integers a1,a2,…,ana_1, a_2, \dots, a_na1​,a2​,…,an​.
The third line contains nnn space-separated integers w1,w2,…,wnw_1, w_2, \dots, w_nw1​,w2​,…,wn​.


Output Format
Output one integer: the minimum possible biomass achievable.


Subtasks
The input is divided into multiple subtasks. You will get the points allocated for a subtask if and only if you solve every testcase in the subtask correctly, within the time limit.


Please remember that when you make a submission to a problem, we will automatically consider your submission for every single subtask, and also the full problem. After the contest, your scores will be 'stitched'. This means that if you solve a subtask in any of your submissions, you will get the points for the subtask in your final score. There is no need to combine your solutions for different subtasks into the same submission.


**Participants are reminded that the answers for this problem might exceed the range of a 32-bit integer, and to use 64-bit integers in computation if necessary.**


For all subtasks:



n≥1n \geq 1n≥1, k≥0k \geq 0k≥0
k<n≤2500k \lt n \leq 2500k<n≤2500
1≤ai≤25001 \leq a_i \leq 25001≤ai​≤2500 for all iii
1≤wi≤1091 \leq w_i \leq 10^91≤wi​≤109 for all iii

These are the subtasks for the problem:



Subtask 1 [8 points]: k=0k=0k=0, n≤25n \leq 25n≤25
Subtask 2 [13 points]: k=1k=1k=1, n≤25n \leq 25n≤25
Subtask 3 [9 points]: k≤10k \leq 10k≤10, n≤25n \leq 25n≤25
Subtask 4 [15 points]: n≤50n \leq 50n≤50
Subtask 5 [15 points]: n≤500n \leq 500n≤500
Subtask 6 [18 points]: wi≤wi+1w_i \leq w_{i+1}wi​≤wi+1​ for all iii
Subtask 7 [22 points]: No additional constraints.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
It is optimal to place walls at i=2i=2i=2, i=3i=3i=3, i=4i=4i=4, and i=6i=6i=6. The total weight of animals in the first segment is (1+4)⋅7=35(1+4) \cdot 7 = 35(1+4)⋅7=35. The total weight of animals in the second segment is 3⋅2=63 \cdot 2 = 63⋅2=6. The total weight of animals in the third segment is 2⋅9=182 \cdot 9 = 182⋅9=18. The total weight of animals in the fourth segment is (4+5)⋅3=27(4+5) \cdot 3 = 27(4+5)⋅3=27. The total weight of animals in the final segment is 1⋅5=51 \cdot 5 = 51⋅5=5. The total biomass in this example is 35+6+18+27+5=9135+6+18+27+5 = 9135+6+18+27+5=91.
